The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[burial mound]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[burial mound]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>
==References== {{reflist}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[burial mound]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[burial mound]] was built.

In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[burial mound]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[burial mound]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[burial mound]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>
==References== {{reflist}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[burial mound]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[burial mound]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[burial mound]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>
==References== {{reflist}}

Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
