revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = Kamayama-jinja Haiden.jpg | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34.2009|135.2046|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Itsuse no Mikoto]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = ''[[Kasuga-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 13. }} [[file:Kamayamano-haka-2.jpg|right|thumb|270px|Kamayama ''Kofun'']] {{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[Sengoku period]] when [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[daimyō]]'' of [[Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest rank to the highest range in the ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kamayama-jinja}} *[http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052 Wakayama Jinjacho] official website

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11597743]] Wikidata name: Kamayama Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11597743]] Wikidata name: Kamayama Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = File:Kamayama-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.2009|N|135.205|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Hiko Itsuse no Mikoto|qid=Q10881970}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|qid=Q3193781}} | festival = October 13. | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|qid=Q200747}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|qid=Q10444949}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[:en:Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[:en:Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] when [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[:en:Asano Yoshinaga|Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[:en:Tokugawa Yorinobu|Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[:en:Kamayama Station|Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[:en:Kishigawa Line|Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Kamayama-jinja}} *[http://www.wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052 Wakayama Jinjacho] official website

However, with the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[:en:Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[:en:Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] when [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[:en:Asano Yoshinaga|Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[:en:Tokugawa Yorinobu|Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[:en:Kamayama Station|Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[:en:Kishigawa Line|Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11597743]] Wikidata name: Kamayama Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11597743]] Wikidata name: Kamayama Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = File:Kamayama-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.2009|N|135.205|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Hiko Itsuse no Mikoto|WD=Q10881970}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = October 13. | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[:en:Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[:en:Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] when [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[:en:Asano Yoshinaga|Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[:en:Tokugawa Yorinobu|Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[:en:Kamayama Station|Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[:en:Kishigawa Line|Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>
==References== {{reflist}}

In 1669 [[:en:Tokugawa Yorinobu|Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[:en:Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[:en:Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] when [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[:en:Asano Yoshinaga|Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[:en:Tokugawa Yorinobu|Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11597743]] Wikidata name: Kamayama Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11597743]] Wikidata name: Kamayama Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = File:Kamayama-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.2009|N|135.205|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Hiko Itsuse no Mikoto|WD=Q10881970}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = October 13. | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[:en:Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[:en:Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] when [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[:en:Asano Yoshinaga|Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[:en:Tokugawa Yorinobu|Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[:en:Kamayama Station|Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[:en:Kishigawa Line|Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[:en:Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[:en:Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] when [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[:en:Asano Yoshinaga|Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[:en:Tokugawa Yorinobu|Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[:en:Kamayama Station|Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[:en:Kishigawa Line|Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11597743]] Wikidata name: Kamayama Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11597743]] Wikidata name: Kamayama Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = File:Kamayama-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.2009|N|135.205|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Hiko Itsuse no Mikoto|WD=Q10881970}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = October 13. | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]], the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the [[:en:Nara Basin|Yamato Basin]], Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near [[:en:Naniwa-ku, Osaka|Naniwa]]. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] when [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]'s forces invaded [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]], the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial household]]. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by [[:en:Asano Yoshinaga|Asano Yoshinaga]], albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 [[:en:Tokugawa Yorinobu|Tokugawa Yorinobu]], the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū Domain]] sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by [[:en:Kamayama Station|Kamayama Station]] on the Wakayama Electric Railway [[:en:Kishigawa Line|Kishigawa Line]].<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency=[[:en:The Asahi Shimbun|The Asahi Shimbun]] |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the city of {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|WD=Q200747|lt=Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}, the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Yamato Basin}}, Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa}}. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} when {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s forces invaded {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}, the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial household}}. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by {{ill|Asano Yoshinaga|en|Asano Yoshinaga|WD=Q1143577|lt=Asano Yoshinaga}}, albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 {{ill|Tokugawa Yorinobu|en|Tokugawa Yorinobu|WD=Q1045763|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|WD=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain}} sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11597743]] Wikidata name: Kamayama Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11597743]] Wikidata name: Kamayama Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = File:Kamayama-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.2009|N|135.205|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Hiko Itsuse no Mikoto|WD=Q10881970}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = October 13. | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the city of {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|WD=Q200747|lt=Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}, the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Yamato Basin}}, Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa}}. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} when {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s forces invaded {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}, the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial household}}. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by {{ill|Asano Yoshinaga|en|Asano Yoshinaga|WD=Q1143577|lt=Asano Yoshinaga}}, albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 {{ill|Tokugawa Yorinobu|en|Tokugawa Yorinobu|WD=Q1045763|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|WD=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain}} sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}, [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by {{ill|Kamayama Station|en|Kamayama Station|WD=Q11597742|lt=Kamayama Station}} on the Wakayama Electric Railway {{ill|Kishigawa Line|en|Kishigawa Line|WD=Q6416586|lt=Kishigawa Line}}.<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun}} |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by {{ill|Kamayama Station|en|Kamayama Station|WD=Q11597742|lt=Kamayama Station}} on the Wakayama Electric Railway {{ill|Kishigawa Line|en|Kishigawa Line|WD=Q6416586|lt=Kishigawa Line}}.<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun}} |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11597743]] Wikidata name: Kamayama Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11597743]] Wikidata name: Kamayama Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = File:Kamayama-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.2009|N|135.205|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Hiko Itsuse no Mikoto|WD=Q10881970}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = October 13. | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the city of {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|WD=Q200747|lt=Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}, the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Yamato Basin}}, Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa}}. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} when {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s forces invaded {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}, the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial household}}. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by {{ill|Asano Yoshinaga|en|Asano Yoshinaga|WD=Q1143577|lt=Asano Yoshinaga}}, albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 {{ill|Tokugawa Yorinobu|en|Tokugawa Yorinobu|WD=Q1045763|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|WD=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain}} sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}, [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by {{ill|Kamayama Station|en|Kamayama Station|WD=Q11597742|lt=Kamayama Station}} on the Wakayama Electric Railway {{ill|Kishigawa Line|en|Kishigawa Line|WD=Q6416586|lt=Kishigawa Line}}.<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun}} |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}, [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by {{ill|Kamayama Station|en|Kamayama Station|WD=Q11597742|lt=Kamayama Station}} on the Wakayama Electric Railway {{ill|Kishigawa Line|en|Kishigawa Line|WD=Q6416586|lt=Kishigawa Line}}.<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun}} |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]]
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = File:Kamayama-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.2009|N|135.205|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Hiko Itsuse no Mikoto|WD=Q10881970}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = October 13. | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the city of {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|WD=Q200747|lt=Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}, the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Yamato Basin}}, Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa}}. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} when {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s forces invaded {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}, the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial household}}. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by {{ill|Asano Yoshinaga|en|Asano Yoshinaga|WD=Q1143577|lt=Asano Yoshinaga}}, albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 {{ill|Tokugawa Yorinobu|en|Tokugawa Yorinobu|WD=Q1045763|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|WD=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain}} sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}, [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by {{ill|Kamayama Station|en|Kamayama Station|WD=Q11597742|lt=Kamayama Station}} on the Wakayama Electric Railway {{ill|Kishigawa Line|en|Kishigawa Line|WD=Q6416586|lt=Kishigawa Line}}.<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun}} |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}, [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by {{ill|Kamayama Station|en|Kamayama Station|WD=Q11597742|lt=Kamayama Station}} on the Wakayama Electric Railway {{ill|Kishigawa Line|en|Kishigawa Line|WD=Q6416586|lt=Kishigawa Line}}.<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun}} |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kamayama-jinja]][[en:Kamayama Shrine]][[fr:Sanctuaire Kamayama]][[ja:竈山神社]][[simple:Kamayama Shrine]]
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = File:Kamayama-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.2009|N|135.205|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Hiko Itsuse no Mikoto|WD=Q10881970}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781}} | festival = October 13. | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the city of {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|WD=Q200747|lt=Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}, the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Yamato Basin}}, Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa}}. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} when {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s forces invaded {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}}, the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial household}}. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by {{ill|Asano Yoshinaga|en|Asano Yoshinaga|WD=Q1143577|lt=Asano Yoshinaga}}, albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 {{ill|Tokugawa Yorinobu|en|Tokugawa Yorinobu|WD=Q1045763|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|WD=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain}} sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}}, [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by {{ill|Kamayama Station|en|Kamayama Station|WD=Q11597742|lt=Kamayama Station}} on the Wakayama Electric Railway {{ill|Kishigawa Line|en|Kishigawa Line|WD=Q6416586|lt=Kishigawa Line}}.<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun}} |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

However, with the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}, [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
