{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = File:Kamayama-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.2009|N|135.205|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Hiko Itsuse no Mikoto|WD=Q10881970|1=Itsuse no Mikoto}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|WD=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} | festival = October 13. | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747|1=Wakayama (city)}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the city of {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|WD=Q200747|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama (city)}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Yamato Basin|1=Nara Basin}}, Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa|1=Naniwa-ku, Osaka}}. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} when {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s forces invaded {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}, the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial household|1=Imperial Household Agency}}. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by {{ill|Asano Yoshinaga|en|Asano Yoshinaga|WD=Q1143577|lt=Asano Yoshinaga|1=Asano Yoshinaga}}, albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 {{ill|Tokugawa Yorinobu|en|Tokugawa Yorinobu|WD=Q1045763|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|WD=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain|1=Kishū Domain}} sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}, [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by {{ill|Kamayama Station|en|Kamayama Station|WD=Q11597742|lt=Kamayama Station|1=Kamayama Station}} on the Wakayama Electric Railway {{ill|Kishigawa Line|en|Kishigawa Line|WD=Q6416586|lt=Kishigawa Line|1=Kishigawa Line}}.<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun|1=The Asahi Shimbun}} |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} when {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s forces invaded {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}, the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial household|1=Imperial Household Agency}}. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by {{ill|Asano Yoshinaga|en|Asano Yoshinaga|WD=Q1143577|lt=Asano Yoshinaga|1=Asano Yoshinaga}}, albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 {{ill|Tokugawa Yorinobu|en|Tokugawa Yorinobu|WD=Q1045763|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|WD=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain|1=Kishū Domain}} sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}, [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.

==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by {{ill|Kamayama Station|en|Kamayama Station|WD=Q11597742|lt=Kamayama Station|1=Kamayama Station}} on the Wakayama Electric Railway {{ill|Kishigawa Line|en|Kishigawa Line|WD=Q6416586|lt=Kishigawa Line|1=Kishigawa Line}}.<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun|1=The Asahi Shimbun}} |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the city of {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|WD=Q200747|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama (city)}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|WD=Q10942269|lt=Yamato Basin|1=Nara Basin}}, Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|WD=Q861618|lt=Naniwa|1=Naniwa-ku, Osaka}}. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} when {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s forces invaded {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}, the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial household|1=Imperial Household Agency}}. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by {{ill|Asano Yoshinaga|en|Asano Yoshinaga|WD=Q1143577|lt=Asano Yoshinaga|1=Asano Yoshinaga}}, albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 {{ill|Tokugawa Yorinobu|en|Tokugawa Yorinobu|WD=Q1045763|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|WD=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain|1=Kishū Domain}} sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}, [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by {{ill|Kamayama Station|en|Kamayama Station|WD=Q11597742|lt=Kamayama Station|1=Kamayama Station}} on the Wakayama Electric Railway {{ill|Kishigawa Line|en|Kishigawa Line|WD=Q6416586|lt=Kishigawa Line|1=Kishigawa Line}}.<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|WD=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun|1=The Asahi Shimbun}} |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in the city of Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture, Japan}} {{more citations needed|date=January 2021}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kamayama Shrine | native_name = 竈山神社 | image = File:Kamayama-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Kamayama Jinja Haiden | map_type = Japan Wakayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.2009|N|135.205|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kamayama Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Hiko Itsuse no Mikoto|qid=Q10881970|1=Itsuse no Mikoto}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 438 Wada, Wakayama-shi, Wakayama-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=1052}} | architecture_style = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|qid=Q3193781|1=Kasuga-zukuri}} | festival = October 13. | prefecture= {{ill|Wakayama|qid=Q200747|1=Wakayama (city)}} | part_of= {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|qid=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Kamayama Shrine'''|竈山神社|Kamayama jinja}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the city of {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|qid=Q200747|lt=Wakayama|1=Wakayama (city)}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan.
==History== The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at the Kamayama Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Itsuse no Mikoto]]|彦五瀬命||}}, the eldest brother of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, the legendary first emperor of Japan. Per the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] to conquer the {{ill|Nara Basin|en|Nara Basin|qid=Q10942269|lt=Yamato Basin|1=Nara Basin}}, Jimmu and his brothers forces advanced up the [[Seto Inland Sea]] and landed near {{ill|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|en|Naniwa-ku, Osaka|qid=Q861618|lt=Naniwa|1=Naniwa-ku, Osaka}}. They faced stiff resistance from a local king named Nagamitsu-hiko and during the ensuing battle Itsuse no Mikoto was struck by an arrow. He advised his brothers that since they were the descendants of [[Amaterasu]], the sun goddess, it was inauspicious that they fight towards the rising sun, and urged that the army should circle around and attack Nagamitsu-hiko from behind (i.e. towards the west). The army thus turned south into the [[Kii Peninsula]], but Itsuse no Mikoto's wound worsened, and he died at a place called "Kameyama", where his [[Kofun]] was built.
As all documentary records of Kameyama Shrine were lost during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} when {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s forces invaded {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|qid=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}, the foundation of the shrine is uncertain. Its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, by which time it was a substantial shrine, and the ''[[kofun]]'' located behind the ''[[Honden]]'' had been identified as the tomb of Itsuse no Mikoto by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial household|1=Imperial Household Agency}}. In records dated 1381, the head ''[[kannushi]]'' of the shrine was a hereditary position held by the Ukai family, who claimed to be descendants of the [[Kofun period]] ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Kii Province. The shrine was destroyed in 1585 by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and restored in 1600 by {{ill|Asano Yoshinaga|en|Asano Yoshinaga|qid=Q1143577|lt=Asano Yoshinaga|1=Asano Yoshinaga}}, albeit on a much smaller scale. In 1669 {{ill|Tokugawa Yorinobu|en|Tokugawa Yorinobu|qid=Q1045763|lt=Tokugawa Yorinobu|1=Tokugawa Yorinobu}}, the first ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kishū Domain|en|Kishū Domain|qid=Q1069187|lt=Kishū Domain|1=Kishū Domain}} sponsored the rebuilding of the ''Honden'' and surrounding structures; however, throughout the [[:en:Edo Period|Edo Period]], the shrine was under the control of the domain's ''[[jisha-bugyō]]'' and had no parishioners or outside sources of income.{{cn|date=January 2024}}
Following the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]], the shrine was initially accorded the Rank of village shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1873. However, with the development of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} and increasing emphasis on Emperor Jimmu, the shrine was promoted to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 2nd Rank|官幣中社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and then to {{nihongo|Imperial Shrine, 1st Rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1915. It is the only example of a shrine which was promoted from the lowest Rank to the highest range in the Ranking system. The shrine was extensively remodeled in 1938.
The {{nihongo|Kameyama Tomb|竈山墓||}} is a [[Round Kofun|circular-type]] ({{nihongo|''empun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}}) ''kofun'', with a height of one meter and diameter of six meters, located on a hill with an elevation of nine meters. As it has been designated as an imperial tomb by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}}, [[archaeological excavation]] has been prohibited.
==Gallery== <gallery> Kamayama-jinja otorii.JPG|Ō-Torii Kamayama-jinja shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kamayama-jinja shinmon.JPG|Gate File:Kamayama-jinja sandou.JPG|Approach File:Kamayama-jinja torii.JPG|Torii </gallery>
==Transportation== The shrine can be reached by {{ill|Kamayama Station|en|Kamayama Station|qid=Q11597742|lt=Kamayama Station|1=Kamayama Station}} on the Wakayama Electric Railway {{ill|Kishigawa Line|en|Kishigawa Line|qid=Q6416586|lt=Kishigawa Line|1=Kishigawa Line}}.<ref>{{cite news |title=人の手を借りる、たま駅長 貴志駅（和歌山県、和歌山電鉄貴志川線） |url=http://www.asahi.com/travel/hitoekigatari/TKY201102280171.html |access-date=15 May 2020 |agency={{ill|The Asahi Shimbun|en|The Asahi Shimbun|qid=Q720503|lt=The Asahi Shimbun|1=The Asahi Shimbun}} |date=1 March 2011 |language=ja}}</ref>
